text: The patient is positioned supine with both arms tucked, and the surgical team uses a standard port configuration for robotic right hemicolectomy. A table brake is applied at the hips to increase the distance between the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and the lower border of the ribs. The setup includes a gel point mini in a three-centimeter incision, along with one twelve-millimeter port and two eight-millimeter ports.

The robotic system is utilized to perform the central mesocolic excision using a superior mesenteric vein-first technique. The small bowel is retracted proximally and stabilized with an endoractor. Fenestrated bipolar forceps grasp the mesentery while monopolar curved scissors dissect the peritoneum, exposing the medial aspect of the superior mesenteric vein to remove overlying lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon dissects the iliocolic pedicle, revealing the iliocolic artery positioned posterior to the vein and passing beneath the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Both the iliocolic vein and artery are divided together, although typically each vessel is divided separately.

The surgeon divides named vessels using mechanical fixation followed by an energy device or scissors. Three hemo-lock clips are applied to secure the vessels, and a vessel sealer is used to complete the division.
The surgeon dissects the duodenum away and pushes it posteriorly, continuing the dissection along the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) towards the pancreas.

The surgeon navigates the variations of Henle's trunk and its tributaries, focusing on the configuration where the superior right colic vein joins the right gastroepiploic vein and the anterior superior pancreaticoduodenal vein to form the trunk of Henle. The dissection proceeds along the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) towards the middle colic artery or its right branch.
The duodenum and pancreas are visibly cleared of overlying lymphatic tissue. The surgeon divides the middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon takes the right colic vein, defining the medial boundary of the central mesocolic excision over the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The superior boundary is marked where the gastroepiploic vein meets the trunk of Henle.
The surgeon uses Surgicel Fibrillar to control venous bleeding, avoiding energy devices. The dissection of the duodenum continues in a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the ascending colon. The ileal mesentery is separated using a vessel sealer, followed by the division of the ileum with a SureForm stapler loaded with a 16mm blue cartridge.

The surgeon divides the greater omentum from the transverse colon, then proceeds to divide the transverse colon mesentery. The lesser sac is entered, and the lesser omentum is taken down, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection toward the hepatic flexure using a top-down approach. The colon is divided, and a window is created in the colonic mesentery. A Shaw Forms 60mm blue load cartridge is used to divide the colon.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery, placing the specimen above the liver for retrieval later. Firefly imaging is employed after ICG injection to visually confirm the vascularity of the conduits.

The surgeon positions the bowel for an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis, ensuring the enterotomy on the small bowel is at least two centimeters back from the staple line to facilitate easy closure.

The surgeon places the greater omentum above in the supracolic compartment. A colotomy is made in the tinea of the transverse colon. A sixty millimeter blue load SureForm stapler is positioned, with the small jaw placed in the mobile small bowel first, followed by the large jaw in the large bowel. Care is taken to avoid tearing the colon during this process.

The surgeon aligns the enterotomy and colotomy evenly before firing the stapler to construct the anastomosis. After stapling, the common enterotomy is closed using a 3-0 V-lock suture, with sutures pulled tight at the end of each throw. A second suture is placed at the opposite end, and both sutures are tied together in the middle.
The surgeon completes the intracorporeal anastomosis, which is then covered with the omentum, providing visual reinforcement of the anastomotic site.
